title: Coquilles Saint-Jacques
subtitle: Scallops gratin with white wine, cream, and Gruyère
excerpt: Scallops poached briefly in white wine with shallots, then returned to their shells with a white wine and cream sauce, topped with Gruyère, and grilled until golden. A French bistro classic that is part technique exercise, part occasion dish.
sourceNotes: Coquilles Saint-Jacques au gratin is documented in classical French cuisine from the early twentieth century, appearing in Escoffier's 'Le Guide Culinaire' (1903). The dish is named for Saint James (Jacques), whose scallop shell symbol appears across the Camino de Santiago pilgrimage route. The gratin technique of finishing a creamed shellfish dish under a grill is common across the Norman and Breton coastlines.

content.text: The scallops poach for two minutes, enough to firm them slightly but leave the centre barely set. They finish under the grill inside the cream sauce, and the residual heat from the gratin does the rest. The critical error is overcooking: a scallop that starts rubbery from the initial poach will be bullet-hard after the grill. Remove from the poaching liquid while still translucent at the centre.

If you can buy scallops in their shells, keep the curved (deep) halves for presentation and use them as the gratin dishes. Otherwise, use shallow ramekins or a gratin dish. The dish works either way, but the shells have a drama to them.

items.name: Scallops
items.prepNote: in shells if possible, roe removed or retained

content.text: Heat the grill to its highest setting. Combine the {{dry-white-wine}} of wine, {{shallot}}, and a pinch of salt in a small saucepan. Bring to a simmer. Add the {{scallops}} and poach for 2 minutes, turning once. Remove with a slotted spoon and set aside. Reserve the poaching liquid.

Strain the poaching liquid and reduce in the same pan over high heat to about 75 ml, this concentrates the flavour. Melt the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a separate small saucepan. Stir in the {{plain-flour}} and cook for 1 minute. Gradually whisk in the reduced poaching liquid, then the {{double-cream}} of cream. Simmer gently for 3 minutes, stirring, until the sauce coats the back of a spoon. Season with salt and white pepper.

Place the scallop shells or a gratin dish on a baking tray. Spoon a little sauce into each shell, nestle the poached scallops on top, then cover with the remaining sauce. Mix the {{gruyere}} with the {{breadcrumbs-fresh}} and scatter over the top. Grill for 3 to 4 minutes, watching closely, until the top is golden and bubbling. Serve immediately with {{lemon}} wedges.

items.fix: Poach for 2 minutes maximum while still translucent at centre; the grill adds another minute of cooking, factor this in
items.cause: Poached too long or grilled too long

items.fix: Add the liquid in three or four additions, whisking each one in fully before adding more
items.cause: Liquid added too fast to the flour-butter roux

items.fix: Pre-heat the grill fully and position the tray as close to the element as possible; 3 to 4 minutes under a hot grill should colour the top
items.cause: Grill not hot enough, or dish too far from the element

items.fix: Reduce to at least half volume before making the sauce; the wine and shallot reduction is where most of the flavour lives

content.text: Coquilles Saint-Jacques in its gratin form is a postwar French restaurant first course. Cream sauces with shellfish and a cheese gratin defined serious French cooking of that era. The dish sits alongside moules marinières and sole meunière in the bistro canon. Scallop season runs autumn through winter in northern European waters, so it is a cold-months dish. The scallop shell is also a symbol of the medieval Camino de Santiago pilgrimage, which is an unlikely backstory for a Friday-night bistro staple.
